blurb: A low, xeric spreading mat of grey-green foliage that erupts in a late-season blaze of scarlet-orange tubular trumpet flowers from late summer into fall — one of the premier hummingbird plants for a hot, dry, well-drained bank. The Front Range form sold as 'Orange Carpet' is the cold-hardy Rocky Mountain subspecies, far tougher than the tender California types.
size: 4–6 in. × 18–24 in. (a low spreading mat; the hardy 'Orange Carpet' form)
sun: Full sun (afternoon shade OK at hot, low sites)
spread: Spreads by underground rhizomes into a loose colony/mat — give it room or edge it each spring
seasons: Grey-green summer mat; scarlet-orange trumpets late summer into fall (Aug–Oct); dies back to the ground over winter
wildlife: Premier hummingbird plant — the late scarlet trumpets fuel fall migration; also valuable to native bees
toxic: No toxicity documented for people or pets; grown ornamentally, not a food plant
origin: Interior western US mountains — Utah, Idaho, Wyoming, Arizona (garrettii form)
habitat: Dry rocky slopes, ridges and gravelly mountain canyons

care.hardiness: USDA zones 4–8, and the hardiness is the reason to grow this particular form here: 'Orange Carpet' is the cold-hardy Rocky Mountain subspecies (*garrettii*), which Plant Select rates to about zone 3 and to roughly 8,150 ft elevation — far tougher than the tender coastal-California forms of the species, which struggle through Front Range winters. At Boulder's 5b–6a it overwinters reliably in a well-drained site, dying back to the ground each winter and re-emerging in late spring.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring (late April–May) once the soil has warmed, giving the rhizomes a full season to establish before their first winter. Set plants in a sharply drained spot — a slope, raised bed, rock garden, or gravelly bank is ideal on our heavy soils. In containers use a fast-draining gritty or cactus-type mix; pots dry out fast in our arid summers so check them often, and because potted roots are far less cold-hardy than in-ground rhizomes, either sink the pot to its rim in the ground for winter or move it to an unheated shelter and keep it nearly dry. Provide a little supplemental water through the first couple of dry winters while it roots in.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun gives the heaviest bloom and the densest, most compact mat — aim for at least 6 hours of direct sun. At hot, low-elevation, reflected-heat sites some afternoon shade is fine and can even help, but deep shade makes the plant sparse and floppy and cuts flowering.[1][2]
care.soil: Lean, gritty, sharply drained soil is the whole game. It will grow in clay, loam, or sand as long as water drains freely — Plant Select notes it even thrives in disturbed soils — but it does not tolerate heavy, wet, poorly drained ground, especially over winter. On Boulder's alkaline clay, plant on a slope or in a raised, grit-amended bed. It tolerates a wide pH from acidic through alkaline (about 6.0–8.0), so our alkaline soils are no obstacle once drainage is right.[2][5]
care.water: Xeric once established. Water regularly through the first season to root it in, then taper off — mature plants get by on natural precipitation with only an occasional deep soak during the hottest stretches of summer. Overly wet soil, particularly in fall and winter, is the main way to lose this plant. Plant Select does advise a little supplemental winter water during the first couple of dry years while it establishes.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space about 18–24 in. apart; each plant spreads by rhizomes into a loose mat 18–24 in. across and knits together within a couple of seasons. Give it room to run, or plan to slice around the edges each year to keep it in bounds (see maintenance).[1][2]
care.propagation: Easiest by division: lift a clump in spring (or in fall/winter) and cut the rhizome-bearing crown into rooted pieces to replant — the running habit makes this simple, and Plant Select suggests dividing every 5–10 years if a patch outgrows its space. It also roots readily from tip cuttings with several leaves attached, taken in late spring to summer and stuck in a gritty, free-draining mix. Seed works as well — the tiny seeds germinate readily and are best surface-sown for light — though named selections like 'Orange Carpet' are grown from divisions or cuttings to stay true to type.[1][4]
care.bloom: The payoff is a long, late-season show: masses of scarlet-orange, tubular trumpet flowers from mid/late summer into fall (roughly August through October here), arriving just as hummingbirds fuel up for their southward migration. It is one of the latest and most valuable nectar plants in the xeric garden.[1][3]
care.feeding: Little to none. This is a lean-soil plant adapted to poor, rocky ground; rich soil or fertilizer produces lank, floppy growth and fewer flowers. Skip feeding entirely, or top-dress very lightly in spring at most.[1][2]
care.maintenance: Very low. Because it dies back over winter, cut the old stems to the ground in early spring as new growth begins — this tidies the mat and encourages fresh bloom, and trimming the mat back a few inches each year also promotes dense spreading. Its one demand on attention is the running rhizome habit: it expands steadily and can wander into neighbors, so site it where it can roam or slice around the edges each spring with a spade to contain it. Divide if a patch outgrows its spot.[1][2]
care.troubles: Trouble-free in the right dry, well-drained site. The dominant risk is rot: overly wet or heavy soil, especially in winter, causes crown and root death — sharp drainage is the cure. Missouri Botanical Garden also notes occasional slug damage and leaf-spot diseases under wetter conditions. Otherwise it is deer-resistant and largely pest-free.[1][2]
care.companions: Pairs naturally with other xeric, sun-loving Front Range plants — sulphur-flower buckwheat, penstemons, blue grama and other native grasses, rabbitbrush, and silvery sages — for a hot, dry, well-drained bank or rock garden. Its late scarlet bloom is especially striking set against the fall-blooming yellows of buckwheat and rabbitbrush.[1][2]

edible.summary: California fuchsia is grown purely as an ornamental hummingbird plant — it has no documented food use, and no toxicity is recorded for it.[3][4]
edible.caution: There is no recorded culinary or foraging use of California fuchsia (*Epilobium canum*, syn. *Zauschneria*), so treat it as inedible and enjoy it as a nectar plant for hummingbirds rather than as food. On the other side, it is not known to be poisonous — no toxicity to people or pets is documented in the horticultural or botanical literature, and it does not appear on toxic-plant lists — so it is a safe plant to have around children and animals; simply don't eat it. Note that despite the common name it is a true willowherb (*Epilobium*), not a *Fuchsia*.[3][4]
